**Christian fundamentalism**, also known as **fundamental Christianity** or **fundamentalist Christianity**, is a [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") movement emphasizing [biblical literalism](/wiki/Biblical_literalism "Biblical literalism") and [cessationism.](/wiki/Cessationism_versus_continuationism "Cessationism versus continuationism")[[1]](#cite_note-mwebster-1) In its modern form, it began in the late 19th and early 20th centuries among [British](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Protestantism in the United Kingdom") and [American Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_United_States "Protestantism in the United States")[[2]](#cite_note-Marsden_1980,_pp._55-62,_118-23-2) as a reaction to [theological liberalism](/wiki/Liberal_Christianity "Liberal Christianity") and cultural [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism"). [Fundamentalists](/wiki/Fundamentalism "Fundamentalism") argued that [19th-century modernist theologians](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_19th_century#Modernism_in_Christian_theology "Christianity in the 19th century") had misunderstood or rejected certain [doctrines](/wiki/Doctrine "Doctrine"), especially [biblical inerrancy](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy"), which they considered the fundamentals of the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") faith.[[3]](#cite_note-Sandeen-3)

Fundamentalists are almost always described as upholding beliefs in [biblical infallibility](/wiki/Biblical_infallibility "Biblical infallibility") and biblical inerrancy,[[4]](#cite_note-Melton1988-4) in keeping with traditional Christian doctrines concerning [biblical interpretation](/wiki/Exegesis "Exegesis"), the role of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") in the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), and the role of the church in society. Fundamentalists usually believe in a core of Christian beliefs, typically called the "Five Fundamentals". These arose from the [Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church "Presbyterian Church") issuance of "The Doctrinal Deliverance of 1910".[[5]](#cite_note-5) Topics included are statements on the [historical accuracy of the Bible](/wiki/Historicity_of_the_Bible "Historicity of the Bible") and all of the events which are recorded in it as well as the [Second Coming of Jesus Christ](/wiki/Second_Coming_of_Jesus_Christ "Second Coming of Jesus Christ").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Fundamentalism manifests itself in various [denominations](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") which believe in various theologies, rather than a single [denomination](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination") or a [systematic theology](/wiki/Systematic_theology "Systematic theology").[[7]](#cite_note-Zamora1982-7) The ideology became active in the 1910s after the release of *[The Fundamentals](/wiki/The_Fundamentals "The Fundamentals")*, a twelve-volume set of essays, [apologetic](/wiki/Christian_apologetics "Christian apologetics") and [polemic](/wiki/Polemic "Polemic"), written by conservative Protestant theologians in an attempt to defend beliefs which they considered Protestant [orthodoxy](/wiki/Orthodoxy "Orthodoxy"). The movement became more organized within U.S. Protestant churches in the 1920s, especially among [Presbyterians](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism"), as well as [Baptists](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") and [Methodists](/wiki/Methodist "Methodist").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAmmerman1991-8) Many churches which embraced fundamentalism adopted a militant attitude with regard to their core beliefs.[[2]](#cite_note-Marsden_1980,_pp._55-62,_118-23-2) [Reformed fundamentalists](/wiki/Reformed_fundamentalism "Reformed fundamentalism") lay heavy emphasis on historic [confessions of faith](/wiki/Confessions_of_faith "Confessions of faith"), such as the [Westminster Confession of Faith](/wiki/Westminster_Confession_of_Faith "Westminster Confession of Faith"), as well as uphold [Princeton theology](/wiki/Princeton_theology "Princeton theology").[[9]](#cite_note-Carter2019-9) Since 1930, many fundamentalist churches in the Baptist tradition (who generally affirm [dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism")) have been represented by the [Independent Fundamental Churches of America](/wiki/Independent_Fundamental_Churches_of_America "Independent Fundamental Churches of America") (renamed IFCA International in 1996), while many theologically conservative [connexions](/wiki/Connexionalism "Connexionalism") in the Methodist tradition (who adhere to [Wesleyan theology](/wiki/Wesleyan_theology "Wesleyan theology")) align with the [Interchurch Holiness Convention](/wiki/Interchurch_Holiness_Convention "Interchurch Holiness Convention"); in various countries, national bodies such as the [American Council of Christian Churches](/wiki/American_Council_of_Christian_Churches "American Council of Christian Churches") exist to encourage dialogue between fundamentalist bodies of different denominational backgrounds.[[10]](#cite_note-Gasper2020-10) Other fundamentalist denominations have little contact with other bodies.[[11]](#cite_note-Jones2016-11)

A few scholars label [Catholic](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism") activist conservative associations who reject modern [Christian theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") in favor of more traditional doctrines as fundamentalists.[[12]](#cite_note-Keating-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDingesHitchcock1991-13)[[14]](#cite_note-Catholic-14) The term is sometimes mistakenly confused with the term *[evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism")*.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The term *fundamentalism* entered the English language in 1922, and it is often capitalized when it is used in reference to the religious movement.[[1]](#cite_note-mwebster-1) By the end of the 20th century, the term *fundamentalism* acquired a pejorative connotation, denoting [religious fanaticism](/wiki/Religious_fanaticism "Religious fanaticism") or [extremism](/wiki/Extremism "Extremism"), especially when such [labeling](/wiki/Labelling#Labelling_in_argumentation "Labelling") extended beyond the original movement which coined the term and those who self-identify as fundamentalists.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Some who hold certain, but not all beliefs in common with the original fundamentalist movement reject the label *fundamentalism*, due to its perceived [pejorative](/wiki/Pejorative "Pejorative") nature, while others consider it a banner of pride. In certain parts of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), using the term *fundamentalist* with the intent to stir up religious hatred is a violation of the *[Racial and Religious Hatred Act](/wiki/Racial_and_Religious_Hatred_Act_2006 "Racial and Religious Hatred Act 2006")* of 2006.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The movement has its origins in 1878 in a meeting of the "Believers' Meeting for Bible Study" ([Niagara Bible Conference](/wiki/Niagara_Bible_Conference "Niagara Bible Conference")) in the United States, where 14 fundamental beliefs were established by evangelical pastors.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Fundamentalism draws from multiple traditions in British and American theologies during the 19th century.[[18]](#cite_note-18) According to authors Robert D. Woodberry and Christian S. Smith,

> Following the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"), tensions developed between Northern evangelical leaders over [Darwinism](/wiki/Darwinism "Darwinism") and higher [biblical criticism](/wiki/Biblical_criticism "Biblical criticism"); Southerners remained unified in their opposition to both. ... Modernists attempted to update [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") to match their view of science. They denied biblical miracles and argued that God manifests himself through the social evolution of society. Conservatives resisted these changes. These latent tensions rose to the surface after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in what came to be called the [fundamentalist/modernist split](/wiki/Fundamentalist%E2%80%93Modernist_controversy "Fundamentalist–Modernist controversy").[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19)

However, the split does not mean that there were just two groups: modernists and fundamentalists. There were also people who considered themselves neo-evangelicals, separating themselves from the extreme components of fundamentalism. These neo-evangelicals also wanted to separate themselves from both the fundamentalist movement and the mainstream evangelical movement due to their anti-intellectual approaches.[[19]](#cite_note-:0-19)

From 1910 until 1915, a series of essays titled *[The Fundamentals: A Testimony to the Truth](/wiki/The_Fundamentals "The Fundamentals")* was published by the Testimony Publishing Company of Chicago.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The Northern Presbyterian Church (now [Presbyterian Church in the United States of America](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Presbyterian Church in the United States of America")) influenced the movement with the definition of the [five "fundamentals"](/wiki/Fundamentalist%E2%80%93modernist_controversy#The_Doctrinal_Deliverance_of_1910_(the_Five_Fundamentals) "Fundamentalist–modernist controversy") in 1910, namely

1. The [inspiration](/wiki/Biblical_inspiration "Biblical inspiration") of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") by the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") and the [inerrancy](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy") of Scripture as a result of this.
2. The [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") of Christ.
3. The belief that Christ's death was an [atonement](/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity "Atonement in Christianity") for sin.
4. The bodily [resurrection of Christ](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Christ "Resurrection of Christ").
5. The historical reality of [Christ's miracles](/wiki/Miracles_attributed_to_Jesus "Miracles attributed to Jesus").[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-Sweeney-2005-165-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg/250px-Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)](/wiki/File:Princeton_Theological_Seminary.jpg)

Princeton Seminary in the 19th century

The [Princeton theology](/wiki/Princeton_theology "Princeton theology"), which responded to [higher criticism](/wiki/Higher_criticism "Higher criticism") of the Bible by developing from the 1840s to 1920 the doctrine of inerrancy, was another influence in the movement. This doctrine, also called biblical inerrancy, stated that the Bible was divinely inspired, religiously authoritative, and without error.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) The [Princeton Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary") professor of theology [Charles Hodge](/wiki/Charles_Hodge "Charles Hodge") insisted that the Bible was inerrant because God inspired or "breathed" his exact thoughts into the biblical writers ([2 Timothy 3](/wiki/2_Timothy_3 "2 Timothy 3"):16). Princeton theologians believed that the Bible should be read differently than any other historical document, and they also believed that Christian [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") and [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") led people to [Hell](/wiki/Hell_in_Christianity "Hell in Christianity") just like non-Christian religions did.[[27]](#cite_note-Kee_1998_484-27)

Biblical inerrancy was a particularly significant rallying point for fundamentalists.[[28]](#cite_note-28) This approach to the Bible is associated with [conservative](/wiki/Conservatism "Conservatism") evangelical [hermeneutical](/wiki/Hermeneutics "Hermeneutics") approaches to Scripture, ranging from the [historical-grammatical method](/wiki/Historical-grammatical_method "Historical-grammatical method") to [biblical literalism](/wiki/Biblical_literalism "Biblical literalism").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The [Dallas Theological Seminary](/wiki/Dallas_Theological_Seminary "Dallas Theological Seminary"), founded in 1924 in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), would have a considerable influence in the movement by training students who will establish various independent [Bible Colleges](/wiki/Bible_College "Bible College") and fundamentalist churches in the southern United States.[[30]](#cite_note-Samuel_S._Hill_2006,_p._77-30)

In the 1930s, fundamentalism was viewed by many as a "last gasp" vestige of something from the past[[31]](#cite_note-31) but more recently,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] scholars have shifted away from that view.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In the early 1940s, evangelicals and fundamentalist Christians began to part ways over whether to separate from modern culture (the fundamentalist approach) or engage with it.[[34]](#cite_note-split-beliefnet-34) An organization very much on the side of separation from modernity was the [American Council of Christian Churches](/wiki/American_Council_of_Christian_Churches "American Council of Christian Churches"), founded in 1941 by Rev. [Carl McIntire](/wiki/Carl_McIntire "Carl McIntire"). Another group "for conservative Christians who wanted to be culturally engaged" was the [National Association of Evangelicals](/wiki/National_Association_of_Evangelicals "National Association of Evangelicals") (NAE) founded in 1942, by [Harold Ockenga](/wiki/Harold_Ockenga "Harold Ockenga").[[34]](#cite_note-split-beliefnet-34)

## Changing interpretations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Changing interpretations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Christian_Demonstrator_Preaching_at_Bele_Chere_2007.jpg/250px-Christian_Demonstrator_Preaching_at_Bele_Chere_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christian_Demonstrator_Preaching_at_Bele_Chere_2007.jpg)

A Christian demonstrator preaching at [Bele Chere](/wiki/Bele_Chere "Bele Chere")

The interpretations given the fundamentalist movement have changed over time, with most older interpretations being based on the concepts of social displacement or cultural lag.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) Some in the 1930s, including [H. Richard Niebuhr](/wiki/H._Richard_Niebuhr "H. Richard Niebuhr"), understood the conflict between fundamentalism and modernism to be part of a broader social conflict between the cities and the country.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) In this view the fundamentalists were country and small-town dwellers who were reacting against the progressivism of city dwellers.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) Fundamentalism was seen as a form of anti-intellectualism during the 1950s; in the early 1960s American intellectual and historian [Richard Hofstadter](/wiki/Richard_Hofstadter "Richard Hofstadter") interpreted it in terms of status anxiety, social displacement, and 'Manichean mentality'.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Beginning in the late 1960s, the movement began to be seen as "a bona fide religious, theological and even intellectual movement in its own right".[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) Instead of interpreting fundamentalism as a simple [anti-intellectualism](/wiki/Anti-intellectualism "Anti-intellectualism"), Paul Carter argued that "fundamentalists were simply intellectual in a way different than their opponents".[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) Moving into the 1970s, Earnest R. Sandeen saw fundamentalism as arising from the confluence of Princeton theology and [millennialism](/wiki/Millennialism "Millennialism").[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32)

[George Marsden](/wiki/George_Marsden "George Marsden") defined fundamentalism as "militantly anti-modernist Protestant evangelicalism" in his 1980 work *Fundamentalism and American Culture*.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) *Militant* in this sense does not mean 'violent', it means 'aggressively active in a cause'.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Marsden saw fundamentalism arising from a number of preexisting evangelical movements that responded to various perceived threats by joining forces.[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32) He argued that Christian [fundamentalists](/wiki/Fundamentalists "Fundamentalists") were American evangelical Christians who in the 20th century opposed "both modernism in theology and the cultural changes that modernism endorsed. Militant opposition to modernism was what most clearly set off fundamentalism."[[37]](#cite_note-37) Others viewing militancy as a core characteristic of the fundamentalist movement include Philip Melling, Ung Kyu Pak and Ronald Witherup.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) [Donald McKim](/wiki/Donald_McKim "Donald McKim") and David Wright (1992) argue that "in the 1920s, militant conservatives (fundamentalists) united to mount a conservative counter-offensive. Fundamentalists sought to rescue their denominations from the growth of modernism at home."[[41]](#cite_note-41)

According to Marsden, recent scholars differentiate "fundamentalists" from "evangelicals" by arguing the former were more militant and less willing to collaborate with groups considered "modernist" in theology. In the 1940s the more moderate faction of fundamentalists maintained the same theology but began calling themselves "evangelicals" to stress their less militant position.[[42]](#cite_note-42) [Roger Olson](/wiki/Roger_Olson "Roger Olson") (2007) identifies a more moderate faction of fundamentalists, which he calls "postfundamentalist", and says "most postfundamentalist evangelicals do not wish to be called fundamentalists, even though their basic theological orientation is not very different". According to Olson, a key event was the formation of the [National Association of Evangelicals](/wiki/National_Association_of_Evangelicals "National Association of Evangelicals") (NAE) in 1942.[[43]](#cite_note-43) Barry Hankins (2008) has a similar view, saying "beginning in the 1940s....militant and separatist evangelicals came to be called fundamentalists, while culturally engaged and non-militant evangelicals were supposed to be called evangelicals."[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Timothy Weber views fundamentalism as "a rather distinctive modern reaction to religious, social and intellectual changes of the late 1800s and early 1900s, a reaction that eventually took on a life of its own and changed significantly over time".[[32]](#cite_note-DOCA_1990-32)

## By region

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: By region")]

### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: North America")]

Fundamentalist movements existed in most North American Protestant denominations by 1919 following attacks on modernist theology in [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian") and [Baptist](/wiki/Baptist "Baptist") denominations. Fundamentalism was especially controversial among Presbyterians.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

#### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Canada")]

[Peter Wiley Philpott](/wiki/Peter_Wiley_Philpott "Peter Wiley Philpott") (1865–1957) founded the United Christian Workers in [Hamilton, Ontario](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario"), in 1892. This [working-class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") religious movement later became the fundamentalist [Associated Gospel Churches of Canada](/wiki/Associated_Gospel_Churches_of_Canada "Associated Gospel Churches of Canada").[[46]](#cite_note-46) In his speech at the 1919 World Conference on Christian Fundamentals in Philadelphia, he asserted that personal experience of conversion cannot be reasoned against or argued away.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

An early fundamentalist leader was English-born [Thomas Todhunter Shields](/wiki/Thomas_Todhunter_Shields "Thomas Todhunter Shields") (1873–1955), who led 80 churches out of the Baptist federation in Ontario in 1927 and formed the Union of Regular Baptist Churches of Ontario and Quebec. He was affiliated with the Baptist Bible Union, based in the United States. His newspaper, *The Gospel Witness,* reached 30,000 subscribers in 16 countries, giving him an international reputation. He was one of the founders of the international Council of Christian Churches.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

[Oswald J. Smith](/wiki/Oswald_J._Smith "Oswald J. Smith") (1889–1986), reared in rural Ontario and educated at [Moody Church](/wiki/Moody_Church "Moody Church") in Chicago, set up The Peoples Church in Toronto in 1928. A dynamic preacher and leader in Canadian fundamentalism, Smith wrote 35 books and engaged in missionary work worldwide. [Billy Graham](/wiki/Billy_Graham "Billy Graham") called him "the greatest combination pastor, hymn writer, missionary statesman, an evangelist of our time."[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: United States")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

English [Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has original text related to this article:

**[The Doctrinal Statement of the World Conference on Christian Fundamentals 1919](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/en:The_Doctrinal_Statement_of_the_World_Conference_on_Christian_Fundamentals_1919 "s:en:The Doctrinal Statement of the World Conference on Christian Fundamentals 1919")**

A leading organizer of the fundamentalist campaign against [modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") in the United States was [William Bell Riley](/wiki/William_Bell_Riley "William Bell Riley"), a [Northern Baptist](/wiki/Northern_Baptist_Convention "Northern Baptist Convention") based in Minneapolis, where his [Northwestern Bible and Missionary Training School](/wiki/University_of_Northwestern_%E2%80%93_St._Paul "University of Northwestern – St. Paul") (1902), Northwestern Evangelical Seminary (1935), and Northwestern College (1944) produced thousands of graduates. At a large conference in Philadelphia in 1919, Riley founded the [World Christian Fundamentals Association](/wiki/World_Christian_Fundamentals_Association "World Christian Fundamentals Association") (WCFA), which became the chief interdenominational fundamentalist organization in the 1920s. Some mark this conference as the public start of Christian fundamentalism.[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) Although the fundamentalist drive to take control of the major Protestant denominations failed at the national level during the 1920s, the network of churches and missions fostered by Riley showed that the movement was growing in strength, especially in [the U.S. South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). Both rural and urban in character, the flourishing movement acted as a denominational surrogate and fostered a militant evangelical Christian orthodoxy. Riley was president of WCFA until 1929, after which the WCFA faded in importance.[[52]](#cite_note-52) The [Independent Fundamental Churches of America](/wiki/Independent_Fundamental_Churches_of_America "Independent Fundamental Churches of America") became a leading association of independent U.S. fundamentalist churches upon its founding in 1930. The [American Council of Christian Churches](/wiki/American_Council_of_Christian_Churches "American Council of Christian Churches") was founded for fundamental Christian denominations as an alternative to the [National Council of Churches](/wiki/National_Council_of_Churches "National Council of Churches").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d1/Machen_Hall%2C_Westminster_Theological_Seminary%2C_Glenside_PA_01.JPG/250px-Machen_Hall%2C_Westminster_Theological_Seminary%2C_Glenside_PA_01.JPG)](/wiki/File:Machen_Hall,_Westminster_Theological_Seminary,_Glenside_PA_01.JPG)

J. Gresham Machen Memorial Hall

Much of the enthusiasm for mobilizing fundamentalism came from Protestant seminaries and Protestant "Bible colleges" in the United States. Two leading fundamentalist seminaries were the dispensationalist [Dallas Theological Seminary](/wiki/Dallas_Theological_Seminary "Dallas Theological Seminary"), founded in 1924 by [Lewis Sperry Chafer](/wiki/Lewis_Sperry_Chafer "Lewis Sperry Chafer"), and the [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_theology#Theology "Reformed theology") [Westminster Theological Seminary](/wiki/Westminster_Theological_Seminary "Westminster Theological Seminary"), formed in 1929 under the leadership and funding of former [Princeton Theological Seminary](/wiki/Princeton_Theological_Seminary "Princeton Theological Seminary") professor [J. Gresham Machen](/wiki/J._Gresham_Machen "J. Gresham Machen").[[53]](#cite_note-53) Many Bible colleges were modeled after the [Moody Bible Institute](/wiki/Moody_Bible_Institute "Moody Bible Institute") in Chicago. [Dwight Moody](/wiki/Dwight_Moody "Dwight Moody") was influential in preaching the imminence of the Kingdom of God that was so important to dispensationalism.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Bible colleges prepared ministers who lacked college or seminary experience with intense study of the Bible, often using the *[Scofield Reference Bible](/wiki/Scofield_Reference_Bible "Scofield Reference Bible")* of 1909, a [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version") of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") with detailed notes which interprets passages from a dispensational perspective.

Although U.S. fundamentalism began in the [North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States"), the movement's largest base of popular support was in the South, especially among [Southern Baptists](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention"), where individuals (and sometimes entire churches) left the convention and joined other Baptist denominations and movements which they believed were "more conservative" such as the [Independent Baptist](/wiki/Independent_Baptist "Independent Baptist") movement. By the late 1920s the national media had identified it with the South, largely ignoring manifestations elsewhere.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In the mid-twentieth century, several Methodists left the mainline [Methodist Church](/wiki/Methodist_Church_(USA) "Methodist Church (USA)") and established fundamental Methodist denominations, such as the [Evangelical Methodist Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Methodist_Church "Evangelical Methodist Church") and the [Fundamental Methodist Conference](/wiki/Fundamental_Methodist_Conference,_Inc. "Fundamental Methodist Conference, Inc.") (cf. *[conservative holiness movement](/wiki/Conservative_holiness_movement "Conservative holiness movement")*); others preferred congregating in Independent Methodist churches, many of which are affiliated with the [Association of Independent Methodists](/wiki/Association_of_Independent_Methodists "Association of Independent Methodists"), which is fundamentalist in its theological orientation.[[56]](#cite_note-Crespino2007-56) By the 1970s Protestant fundamentalism was deeply entrenched and concentrated in the U.S. South. In 1972–1980 [General Social Surveys](/wiki/General_Social_Survey "General Social Survey"), 65 percent of respondents from the "East South Central" region (comprising [Tennessee](/wiki/Tennessee "Tennessee"), [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"), [Mississippi](/wiki/Mississippi "Mississippi"), and [Alabama](/wiki/Alabama "Alabama")) self-identified as fundamentalist. The share of fundamentalists was at or near 50 percent in "West South Central" ([Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") to [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas")) and "South Atlantic" (Florida to Maryland), and at 25 percent or below elsewhere in the country, with the low of nine percent in New England. The pattern persisted into the 21st century; in 2006–2010 surveys, the average share of fundamentalists in the East South Central Region stood at 58 percent, while, in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), it climbed slightly to 13 percent.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

##### Evolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Evolution")]

In the 1920s, Christian fundamentalists "differed on how to understand the account of creation in Genesis" but they "agreed that God was the author of creation and that humans were distinct creatures, separate from animals, and made in the image of God."[[58]](#cite_note-Sutton2019-58) While some of them advocated the belief in [Old Earth creationism](/wiki/Old_Earth_creationism "Old Earth creationism") and a few of them even advocated the belief in [evolutionary creation](/wiki/Evolutionary_creation "Evolutionary creation"), other "strident fundamentalists" advocated [Young Earth Creationism](/wiki/Young_Earth_Creationism "Young Earth Creationism") and "associated evolution with last-days atheism."[[58]](#cite_note-Sutton2019-58) These "strident fundamentalists" in the 1920s devoted themselves to fighting against the [teaching of evolution](/wiki/Teaching_of_evolution "Teaching of evolution") in the nation's schools and colleges, especially by passing state laws that affected public schools. [William Bell Riley](/wiki/William_Bell_Riley "William Bell Riley") took the initiative in the 1925 [Scopes Trial](/wiki/Scopes_Trial "Scopes Trial") by bringing in famed politician [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan") and hiring him to serve as an assistant to the local prosecutor, who helped draw national media attention to the trial. In the half century after the Scopes Trial, fundamentalists had little success in shaping government policy, and they were generally defeated in their efforts to reshape the [mainline denominations](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant"), which refused to join fundamentalist attacks on evolution.[[27]](#cite_note-Kee_1998_484-27) Particularly after the Scopes Trial, liberals saw a division between Christians in favor of the teaching of evolution, whom they viewed as educated and tolerant, and Christians against evolution, whom they viewed as narrow-minded, tribal, and obscurantist.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Edwards (2000), however, challenges the consensus view among scholars that in the wake of the Scopes trial, fundamentalism retreated into the political and cultural background, a viewpoint which is evidenced in the movie [*Inherit the Wind*](/wiki/Inherit_the_Wind_(1960_film) "Inherit the Wind (1960 film)") and the majority of contemporary historical accounts. Rather, he argues, the cause of fundamentalism's retreat was the death of its leader, Bryan. Most fundamentalists saw the trial as a victory rather than a defeat, but Bryan's death soon afterward created a leadership void that no other fundamentalist leader could fill. Unlike the other fundamentalist leaders, Bryan brought name recognition, respectability, and the ability to forge a broad-based coalition of fundamentalist religious groups to argue in favor of the anti-evolutionist position.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

Gatewood (1969) analyzes the transition from the anti-evolution crusade of the 1920s to the [creation science](/wiki/Creation_science "Creation science") movement of the 1960s. Despite some similarities between these two causes, the creation science movement represented a shift from religious to [pseudoscientific](/wiki/Pseudoscience "Pseudoscience") objections to Darwin's theory. Creation science also differed in terms of popular leadership, rhetorical tone, and sectional focus. It lacked a prestigious leader like Bryan, utilized pseudoscientific argument rather than religious rhetoric, and was a product of California and Michigan rather than the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Webb (1991) traces the political and legal struggles between strict creationists and Darwinists to influence the extent to which evolution would be taught as science in Arizona and California schools. After Scopes was convicted, creationists throughout the United States sought similar anti-evolution laws for their states. These included Reverends R. S. Beal and Aubrey L. Moore in Arizona and members of the Creation Research Society in California, all supported by distinguished laymen. They sought to ban evolution as a topic for study, or at least relegate it to the status of unproven theory perhaps taught alongside the biblical version of creation. Educators, scientists, and other distinguished laymen favored evolution. This struggle occurred later in the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") than in other US areas and persisted through the Sputnik era.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

In recent times, the courts have heard cases on whether or not the Book of Genesis's creation account should be taught in science classrooms alongside evolution, most notably in the 2005 federal court case *[Kitzmiller v. Dover Area School District](/wiki/Kitzmiller_v._Dover_Area_School_District "Kitzmiller v. Dover Area School District")*.[[63]](#cite_note-ncse.com-63) Creationism was presented under the banner of [intelligent design](/wiki/Intelligent_design "Intelligent design"), with the book *[Of Pandas and People](/wiki/Of_Pandas_and_People "Of Pandas and People")* being its textbook. The trial ended with the judge deciding that teaching intelligent design in a science class was unconstitutional as it was a religious belief and not science.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

The original fundamentalist movement divided along clearly defined lines within conservative evangelical Protestantism as issues progressed. Many groupings, large and small, were produced by this schism. [Neo-evangelicalism](/wiki/Neo-evangelicalism "Neo-evangelicalism"), the [Heritage movement](/wiki/David_Barton_(author) "David Barton (author)"), and [Paleo-Orthodoxy](/wiki/Paleo-Orthodoxy "Paleo-Orthodoxy") have all developed distinct identities, but none of them acknowledge any more than an historical overlap with the fundamentalist movement, and the term is seldom used of them. The broader term "[evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical")" includes fundamentalists as well as people with similar or identical religious beliefs who do not engage the outside challenge to the Bible as actively.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Writing in 2023, conservative Christian journalist [David French](/wiki/David_French_(political_commentator) "David French (political commentator)") quotes a former president of the [Southern Baptist Convention](/wiki/Southern_Baptist_Convention "Southern Baptist Convention")'s Ethics & Religious Liberty Commission, [Richard Land](/wiki/Richard_Land "Richard Land"), as identifying fundamentalism as "far more a psychology than a theology," with characteristics shared by competing Christian theologies and competing religions. According to French, that psychology is one that shares "three key traits": certainty (of a mind unclouded by doubt), ferocity (against perceived enemies of their religion) and solidarity (of "comrades in the foxhole", a virtue surpassing even piety in importance).[[66]](#cite_note-French-love-7-12-2023-66)

##### Christian right

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Christian right")]

Main article: [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Jerry_Falwell_portrait.jpg/250px-Jerry_Falwell_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jerry_Falwell_portrait.jpg)

[Jerry Falwell](/wiki/Jerry_Falwell "Jerry Falwell"), whose founding of the [Moral Majority](/wiki/Moral_Majority "Moral Majority") was a key step in the formation of the "New Christian Right"

The latter half of the twentieth century witnessed a surge of interest in organized political activism by U.S. fundamentalists. Dispensational fundamentalists viewed the 1948 [establishment of the state of Israel](/wiki/Israeli_Declaration_of_Independence "Israeli Declaration of Independence") as an important sign of the fulfillment of biblical prophecy, and support for Israel became the centerpiece of their approach to U.S. foreign policy.[[67]](#cite_note-67) United States Supreme Court decisions also ignited fundamentalists' interest in organized politics, particularly *[Engel v. Vitale](/wiki/Engel_v._Vitale "Engel v. Vitale")* in 1962, which prohibited state-sanctioned prayer in public schools, and *[Abington School District v. Schempp](/wiki/Abington_School_District_v._Schempp "Abington School District v. Schempp")* in 1963, which prohibited mandatory Bible reading in public schools.[[68]](#cite_note-68) By the time [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") ran for the presidency in 1980, fundamentalist preachers, like the prohibitionist ministers of the early 20th century, were organizing their congregations to vote for supportive candidates.[[69]](#cite_note-69)

Leaders of the newly political fundamentalism included [Rob Grant](/wiki/Robert_Grant_(Christian_leader) "Robert Grant (Christian leader)") and [Jerry Falwell](/wiki/Jerry_Falwell "Jerry Falwell"). Beginning with Grant's American Christian Cause in 1974, [Christian Voice](/wiki/Christian_Voice_(USA) "Christian Voice (USA)") throughout the 1970s and Falwell's [Moral Majority](/wiki/Moral_Majority "Moral Majority") in the 1980s, the [Christian Right](/wiki/Christian_Right "Christian Right") began to have a major impact on American politics. In the 1980s and 1990s, the Christian Right was influencing elections and policy with groups such as the [Family Research Council](/wiki/Family_Research_Council "Family Research Council") (founded 1981 by [James Dobson](/wiki/James_Dobson "James Dobson")) and the [Christian Coalition](/wiki/Christian_Coalition_of_America "Christian Coalition of America") (formed in 1989 by [Pat Robertson](/wiki/Pat_Robertson "Pat Robertson")) helping conservative politicians, especially [Republicans](/wiki/History_of_the_Republican_Party_(United_States) "History of the Republican Party (United States)"), to win state and national elections.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Australia")]

A major organization of fundamentalist, [pentecostal](/wiki/Pentecostal "Pentecostal") churches in Australia is the [International Network of Churches](/wiki/International_Network_of_Churches "International Network of Churches"), formerly known as the "Christian Outreach Centre".[[71]](#cite_note-SMH2-71)

A former influential group was the [Logos Foundation](/wiki/Logos_Foundation_(Australia) "Logos Foundation (Australia)"). The Logos Foundation, led by [Howard Carter](/wiki/Howard_Carter_(evangelist) "Howard Carter (evangelist)"), was a controversial Christian ministry in the 1970s and 1980s that promoted [Reconstructionist](/wiki/Christian_Reconstructionism "Christian Reconstructionism"), [Restorationist](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism"), and [Dominionist](/wiki/Dominion_theology "Dominion theology") theology. They also actively campaigned for several candidates for [Queensland, Australia](/wiki/Queensland,_Australia "Queensland, Australia") public office that shared their values (e.g., anti-abortion). The Logos Foundation disbanded shortly after an adulterous affair by Carter became public in 1990.[[72]](#cite_note-SMH1-72)[[71]](#cite_note-SMH2-71)[[73]](#cite_note-Hey-73)[[74]](#cite_note-Harrison-74)

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main articles: [Pochvennichestvo](/wiki/Pochvennichestvo "Pochvennichestvo"), [Black Hundreds](/wiki/Black_Hundreds "Black Hundreds"), [VSKhSON](/wiki/VSKhSON "VSKhSON"), [Sorok Sorokov Movement](/wiki/Sorok_Sorokov_Movement "Sorok Sorokov Movement"), and [Union of Orthodox Banner-Bearers](/wiki/Union_of_Orthodox_Banner-Bearers "Union of Orthodox Banner-Bearers")

In [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), Christian fundamentalism is often based around the [Russian Orthodox Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Church "Russian Orthodox Church") or the [Russian Orthodox Old-Rite Church](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox_Old-Rite_Church "Russian Orthodox Old-Rite Church"). Orthodox Christian fundamentalism was often connected strongly to a sense of [Russian nationalism](/wiki/Russian_nationalism "Russian nationalism"), since the Russian Orthodox Church often has a strong connection to the [Russian state](/wiki/Government_of_Russia "Government of Russia"). This Church-state connection has arguably existed since the time of [Vladimir the Great](/wiki/Vladimir_the_Great "Vladimir the Great")'s conversion.

In 2013, [composer](/wiki/Composer "Composer") Andrei Kormukhin and [athlete](/wiki/Athlete "Athlete") Vladimir Nosov founded the Orthodox fundamentalist and [conservative Christian](/wiki/Conservative_Christianity "Conservative Christianity") organization known as the [Sorok Sorokov Movement](/wiki/Sorok_Sorokov_Movement "Sorok Sorokov Movement"). The Sorok Sorokov Movement was founded in reaction to [Pussy Riot](/wiki/Pussy_Riot "Pussy Riot")'s 2012 protests, which were themselves against increasingly [socially conservative](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism") policies in Russia, including moves towards [decriminalizing wifebeating](/wiki/Domestic_violence_in_Russia#Decriminalization "Domestic violence in Russia") and [criminalizing homosexuality](/wiki/Russian_anti-LGBTQ_law "Russian anti-LGBTQ law"). The Sorok Sorokov Movement has received support from many [priests](/wiki/Priest "Priest") of the Russian Orthodox Church, most notably [celebrate](/wiki/Clerical_celibacy#Rules_for_Christian_clergy "Clerical celibacy") priest [Vsevolod Chaplin](/wiki/Vsevolod_Chaplin "Vsevolod Chaplin"). Chaplin in particular supported the creation of "Orthodox squads" in order to punish people from carrying out "blasphemous acts" in religious places. Some have argues that the Sorok Sorkov Movement has been involved in protecting the construction of Russian Orthodox churches in [Moscow](/wiki/Moscow "Moscow"), though the facts have been hard to verify with this. Just as many sources have argued that these acts were more in line with violent [vigilantism](/wiki/Vigilantism "Vigilantism") against LGBT people in Russia.[[75]](#cite_note-medium.com-75) The Sorok Sorokov Movement has also been connected to the [Russian far-right](/wiki/Far-right_politics_in_Russia "Far-right politics in Russia"), including [neo-Nazis](/wiki/Neo-Nazism_in_Russia "Neo-Nazism in Russia") and [Third Positionists](/wiki/Third_Position "Third Position").[[75]](#cite_note-medium.com-75) The Sorok Sorokov Movement has its own political party as well, called [For the Family](/wiki/For_the_Family_(political_party) "For the Family (political party)").

Many [far-right Russian](/wiki/Far-right_politics_in_Russia "Far-right politics in Russia") [Christian nationalists](/wiki/Christian_nationalism#Russia "Christian nationalism") have been highly supportive of [Russia's unprovoked war with Ukraine](/wiki/Russo-Ukraine_War "Russo-Ukraine War"). One such group supportive of Russian Orthodox Christian fundamentalist-nationalism is the [Union of Orthodox Banner-Bearers](/wiki/Union_of_Orthodox_Banner-Bearers "Union of Orthodox Banner-Bearers"). Known for their [book burnings](/wiki/Book_burning "Book burning") and [political rallies](/wiki/Political_demonstration "Political demonstration"), their primary goal is to see [a return of the Russian Tsar as supreme autocrat of Russia](/wiki/Monarchism_in_Russia "Monarchism in Russia").[[76]](#cite_note-widerimage.reuters.com-76) The group as a particular affinity for [Tsar Nicolas II](/wiki/Nicolas_II "Nicolas II").[[76]](#cite_note-widerimage.reuters.com-76) The group has at times referred to [Russian president](/wiki/President_of_Russia "President of Russia") [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin") as a modern Tsar, though it is unclear as to whether or not this is a message of support for Putin or not.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

## By denomination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: By denomination")]

### Independent Baptist Churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Independent Baptist Churches")]

Main article: [Independent Baptist](/wiki/Independent_Baptist "Independent Baptist")

Bible Baptist Churches, Fundamental Baptist Churches or Independent Baptist Churches refuse any form of ecclesial authority other than that of the local church.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Great emphasis is placed on the [literal interpretation](/wiki/Literal_interpretation "Literal interpretation") of the Bible as the primary method of Bible study as well as the [biblical inerrancy](/wiki/Biblical_inerrancy "Biblical inerrancy") and the [infallibility](/wiki/Infallibility_of_the_Church "Infallibility of the Church") of their [interpretation](/wiki/Biblical_hermeneutics "Biblical hermeneutics").[[79]](#cite_note-79) [Dispensationalism](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism") is common among Independent Baptists. They are opposed to any [ecumenical](/wiki/Ecumenism "Ecumenism") movement with denominations that do not have the same beliefs.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Many Independent Fundamentalist Baptist (IFB) churches adhere to only using the [King James Version](/wiki/King_James_Version "King James Version"), a position known as [King James Onlyism](/wiki/King_James_Onlyism "King James Onlyism").[[81]](#cite_note-81)

### Methodism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Methodism")]

Further information: [Conservative Holiness Movement](/wiki/Conservative_Holiness_Movement "Conservative Holiness Movement")

Fundamental Methodism includes several [connexions](/wiki/Connexionalism "Connexionalism"), such as the [Evangelical Methodist Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Methodist_Church_of_America "Evangelical Methodist Church of America") and [Fundamental Methodist Conference](/wiki/Fundamental_Methodist_Conference,_Inc. "Fundamental Methodist Conference, Inc."), along with their seminaries such as [Breckbill Bible College](/wiki/Breckbill_Bible_College "Breckbill Bible College").[[82]](#cite_note-82) Additionally, Methodist connexions in the [conservative holiness movement](/wiki/Conservative_holiness_movement "Conservative holiness movement"), such as the [Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection](/wiki/Allegheny_Wesleyan_Methodist_Connection "Allegheny Wesleyan Methodist Connection") and [Evangelical Methodist Church Conference](/wiki/Evangelical_Methodist_Church_Conference "Evangelical Methodist Church Conference"), herald the beliefs of "separation from the world, from false doctrines, from other ecclesiastical connections" as well as place heavy emphasis on practicing [holiness standards](/wiki/Outward_holiness "Outward holiness").[[83]](#cite_note-Graham2013-83)

### Nondenominationalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nondenominationalism")]

In [nondenominational Christianity](/wiki/Nondenominational_Christianity "Nondenominational Christianity") of the [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity") variety, the word *biblical* or *independent* often appears in the name of the church or denomination.[[30]](#cite_note-Samuel_S._Hill_2006,_p._77-30) The independence of the church is claimed and affiliation with a [Christian denomination](/wiki/Christian_denomination "Christian denomination") is infrequent, although there are fundamentalist denominations.[[84]](#cite_note-84)

### Reformed fundamentalism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Reformed fundamentalism")]

Main article: [Reformed fundamentalism](/wiki/Reformed_fundamentalism "Reformed fundamentalism")

Reformed fundamentalism includes those denominations in the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition") (which includes the [Continental Reformed](/wiki/Continental_Reformed "Continental Reformed"), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterian "Presbyterian"), [Reformed Anglican](/wiki/Evangelical_Anglican "Evangelical Anglican") and [Reformed Baptist](/wiki/Reformed_Baptist "Reformed Baptist") Churches) who adhere to the doctrine of [biblical infallibility](/wiki/Biblical_infallibility "Biblical infallibility") and lay heavy emphasis on historic confessions of faith, such as the [Westminster Confession](/wiki/Westminster_Confession "Westminster Confession").[[85]](#cite_note-Dorrien1998-85)[[9]](#cite_note-Carter2019-9)

Examples of Reformed fundamentalist denominations include the [Orthodox Presbyterian Church](/wiki/Orthodox_Presbyterian_Church "Orthodox Presbyterian Church")[[85]](#cite_note-Dorrien1998-85) and the [Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Ulster "Free Presbyterian Church of Ulster").

## Criticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Christian_fundamentalism&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Criticism")]

Fundamentalists' literal interpretation of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") has been criticized by practitioners of [biblical criticism](/wiki/Biblical_criticism "Biblical criticism") for failing to take into account the circumstances in which the Christian Bible was written. Critics claim that this "literal interpretation" is not in keeping with the message which the scripture intended to convey when it was written,[[86]](#cite_note-86) and it also uses the Bible for political purposes by presenting God "more as a God of judgement and punishment than as a God of love and mercy."[[87]](#cite_note-God-87)

In contrast to the higher criticism, fundamentalism claims to keep the Bible open for the people. However, through the complexity of the [dispensational](/wiki/Dispensationalism "Dispensationalism") framework, it has actually forced lay readers to remain dependent upon the inductive methods of Bible teachers and ministers.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

Christian fundamentalism has been linked to [child abuse](/wiki/Child_abuse "Child abuse")[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) as well as [corporal punishment](/wiki/Corporal_punishment "Corporal punishment"),[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) with a number of practitioners believing that the Bible tells them to [spank](/wiki/Spank "Spank") their children.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Artists have addressed the issues of Christian fundamentalism,[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96) with one providing a slogan "America's Premier Child Abuse Brand."[[97]](#cite_note-childabuse-97)

Researchers find evidence anchoring Christian fundamentalism with beliefs in [conspiracy theories](/wiki/Conspiracy_theories "Conspiracy theories") such as [modern flat Earth beliefs](/wiki/Modern_flat_Earth_beliefs "Modern flat Earth beliefs").[[98]](#cite_note-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99) Fundamentalists have attempted and continue to attempt to teach [intelligent design](/wiki/Intelligent_design "Intelligent design"), a hypothesis with [creationism](/wiki/Creationism "Creationism") as its base, in lieu of [evolution](/wiki/Evolution "Evolution") in public schools. This has resulted in legal challenges such as the federal case of *[Kitzmiller v. Dover Area School District](/wiki/Kitzmiller_v._Dover_Area_School_District "Kitzmiller v. Dover Area School District")* which resulted in the [United States District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Middle_District_of_Pennsylvania "United States District Court for the Middle District of Pennsylvania") ruling the teaching of intelligent design to be unconstitutional due to its religious roots.[[100]](#cite_note-100)
